content.full_text:
23 March 1977

Brian Paddock
California Tax Reform Association
1107 Ninth Street - Suite 224
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Paddock:

I have reviewed the data furnished in your letter of March 2, 1977 and have discussed your question with members of the Commission staff. The lobbyist accounting and reporting regulation, 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18615, requires lobbyists, in general, to report "payments to influence legislative or administrative action." Section 82045 of the Political Reform Act defines a "payment to influence legislative or administrative action" as any of the following types of payments:

"(a) Direct or indirect payment to a lobbyist whether for salary, fee, compensation for expenses, or any other purpose, by a person employing or contracting for the services of the lobbyist separately or jointly with other persons;

(b) Payment in support or assistance of a lobbyist or his activities, including but not limited to, the direct payment of expenses incurred at the request or suggestion of the lobbyist;

(c) Payment which directly or indirectly benefits any elective state official, legislative official or agency official or a member of the immediate family of any such official;

(d) Payment, including compensation, payment or reimbursement for the services, time or expenses of an employee, for or in connection with direct communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency official;

(e) Payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter into direct communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency official."

Page 2

Based on the information furnished in your letter, the payments described do not meet the definition of "payments to influence legislative or administrative action" and do not have to be reported on the lobbyist report, form 620.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,


Alan L. Herndon
Compliance Representative

ALH/gt

California Tax Reform Association
1107 Ninth Street — Suite 224
Sacramento, California 95814
(916) 446-1776

San Francisco Bay Area Office:
785 Market Street — Suite 1404
San Francisco, California 94103

2 March 1977

Richard Eichman
Fair Political Practices Committee
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Eichman:

The California Tax Reform Association is a non-profit, unincorporated
association devoted to reformation of the existing tax system. Its
primary source of revenue is from individual memberships. CTRA is
registered as a Lobbyist Employer and has two registered lobbyists.

CTRA is governed by a Board of Directors currently consisting of
eleven persons. Three of the members of this Board are registered lobbyists
who are employed by other registered Lobbyist Employers. All Board
members serve completely as private individuals and give their time to
CTRA as uncompensated volunteers. None of them represent or receive any
instruction from their employers with respect to their participation in the
CTRA or its Board.

The Board sets major policies for CTRA including the ultimate approval or ratification of positions on various general aspects of tax policy as well as
positions on specific bills. The Board also supervises the staff's efforts
in the areas such as membership development, fund raising, public education,
media efforts, and the operation of conferences and publications.

Recently a member was added to the Board who must travel from his home in
Los Angeles to attend Board meetings. Since CTRA operates on a very
small budget, it was decided that all the Board members would contribute from
their personal funds an equal share to pay for round trip plane fare for the
L.A. resident each time he attended. (Note that such an arrangement
actually dilutes each Board member's vote since a larger majority vote
is required to carry a motion. Thus each Board member's influence on
CTRA's legislative policy is reduced.)

Partial List of Sponsors

Janet Adams
Roy Alper
Peter Barnes
William Bennett
Peter Birdsall
Elizabeth Boukas
Senator John Dunlap
Lt. Governor Mervyn Dymally
Assemblywoman Leona Egeland
Secty. of State March Fong Eu

John F. Henning
Assemblyman Larry Kapiloff
Jonathan Kaufman
Jonathan Lewis
Sally Lilienthal
Chuck Mack
Richard Nevins
Kay Pachtner
Brian Paddock

Senator Nicholas C. Petris
William M. Roth
Iris Sankey
Stanley Sheinbaum
Derek Shearer
Senator Jerry Smith
Raoul Teilet
Timothy J. Twomey
State Treasurer Jess Unruh
Assemblyman John Vasconcellos

Richard Eichman
2 March 1977
Page Two.

The question was raised as to whether such a contribution would be a
reportable expense for these Board members who are registered lobbyists.
Such a contribution is purely private and voluntary (our By-laws forbid
assessments.). We believe a nominal and private donation should not be
necessary to report in these circumstances by either the lobbyist or his/her
employer.

The next question was whether or not individual memberships in CTRA were to be
reported by members who are registered lobbyists, whether or not such
members also serve on the Board.

(As I noted in our prior telephone conversation, I was a registered lobbyist
in prior years. It never occurred to me that my voluntary memberships in
organizations which happened to lobby, such ACLU, were reportable.)

Sincerely,

Brian Paddock

Brian Paddock (V6)

BP/kb
